coredata.dc:title: Hinge-like paper-based dual-channel enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescent microfluidic platform for simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yangyu
Tong, Chaoying
Zhou, Rongrong
Qin, Ziyi
Xu, Jinju
Liao, Chunhui
Zhang, Shuihan
Shi, Shuyun
Guo, Ying
coredata.dc:description: On-site multi-pesticide residues detection is particularly urgent and challenging. Here, we fabricated an enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescent detection system in combination with a hinge-like dual-channel 3D microfluidic paper analytical device (3D μPAD) for simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate. Blue-emission 1-naphthol (Em. 470 nm) was hydrolyzed from carbaryl, while yellow-emission 2,3-diaminophenazine (Em. 570 nm) was produced with the aid of Cu2+ for glyphosate sensing. Inner-filter effect between 1-naphthol or 2,3-diaminophenazine and green-emission carbon dots (Em. 510 nm) realized two ratiometric fluorescent detection systems. Remarkable color variation of green–blue for carbaryl (50.00–1100 μΜ) and yellow–green for glyphosate (5.00–600 μΜ) were observed on a dual-channel 3D μPAD without crosstalk. Their detection limits were 1.11 and 0.63 μΜ, respectively. The strategy realized simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in food/herbal with excellent accuracy (spiked recoveries, 91.00–107.2%), high precision (RSD ≤ 8.43%), and superior selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-channel 3D μPAD
Simultaneous visual detection
Ratiometric sensing
Carbaryl
Glyphosate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate using 3D μPAD
Visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples
Operation principles for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate
Mechanism for carbaryl and glyphosate sensing
Simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate by 3D μPAD
Hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Research and Development Projects in Hunan Province, China
Key Research Program of Hunan Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Key Research and Development Projects in Hunan Province, China (2021NK2024), the Key Research Program of Hunan Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine (C2022042, 2021SK2012), and the Special Project of “National Chinese Medica (CMM) Resources Survey” ([2017]66).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (inset: HR-TEM and histogram of size distribution) (A); FT-IR spectrum (B); XPS survey spectrum (C); UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs (D).

Fluorescent spectra (A) and linear relationship (C) of carbaryl (0–55 μM) + OH–+ CDs (50 μg/mL) system; Fluorescent spectra (B) and linear relationship (D) of glyphosate (0–45 μM) + Cu2+ + OPD + CDs (50 μg/mL) system.

Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra for 1-NP and absorption spectrum for CDs (A); Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra for CDs and absorption spectrum for DAP (B); Fluorescence lifetime of 1-NP in the absence and presence of CDs (C); Fluorescence lifetime of CDs before and after addition of DAP (D).

Calibration curve of B/G ratio versus logarithm of carbaryl concentration from 50.00 to 1100 μM (insets: corresponding fluorescent images) (A); Calibration curve of (B + G)/R ratio versus glyphosate concentration from 5.00 to 600 μM (insets: corresponding fluorescent images) (B); CIE chromaticity diagram of the detection zones on the 2nd layer of 3D μPAD for carbaryl (C) and glyphosate (D). The experiments were repeated three times.
Schematic representation of ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy and smartphone-assisted hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD for simultaneous enzyme-free detection of carbaryl and glyphosate.
Detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples by our developed CDs-based ratiometric assay and HPLC/HPLC–MS.

head.ce:title: Hinge-like paper-based dual-channel enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescent microfluidic platform for simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: On-site multi-pesticide residues detection is particularly urgent and challenging. Here, we fabricated an enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescent detection system in combination with a hinge-like dual-channel 3D microfluidic paper analytical device (3D μPAD) for simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate. Blue-emission 1-naphthol (Em. 470 nm) was hydrolyzed from carbaryl, while yellow-emission 2,3-diaminophenazine (Em. 570 nm) was produced with the aid of Cu2+ for glyphosate sensing. Inner-filter effect between 1-naphthol or 2,3-diaminophenazine and green-emission carbon dots (Em. 510 nm) realized two ratiometric fluorescent detection systems. Remarkable color variation of green–blue for carbaryl (50.00–1100 μΜ) and yellow–green for glyphosate (5.00–600 μΜ) were observed on a dual-channel 3D μPAD without crosstalk. Their detection limits were 1.11 and 0.63 μΜ, respectively. The strategy realized simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in food/herbal with excellent accuracy (spiked recoveries, 91.00–107.2%), high precision (RSD ≤ 8.43%), and superior selectivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemical pesticides play an important role in modern agriculture by controlling pests, weeds, and diseases (Li et al., 2022a; Shen, Wei, Zhu, Cao, & Han, 2022). In 2020, worldwide pesticide consumption has increased to 2700 million kilograms (Tabe-Ojong, Nyam, Lokossou, & Gebrekidan, 2023). Broad utilization of pesticides unavoidably possesses serious impact on food safety, environmental contamination, and human health (Li et al., 2022b; Zhao et al., 2021). Carbaryl (1-naphthyl N-methylcarbamate) is the first successful used carbamate insecticide with long residual broad-spectrum property (Liu et al., 2021), while glyphosate (N-(phosphonomethyl) glycine) is a fascinating nonselective organophosphorus herbicide with the largest sales (Niedobová, Ouředníčková, Michalko, & Skalský, 2022). Carbaryl and glyphosate are always mixed-used, which aggravates the issue of food safety due to their synergistic effects (Harischandra et al, 2021; Zhu et al, 2023). Furthermore, both are neurotoxins, which can irreversibly inhibit acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and cause neurotransmitter accumulation in tissues, leading to various neurological diseases and even death (Singh et al., 2020). Accordingly, simultaneous, sensitive, and accurate detection of carbaryl and glyphosate residues in agricultural products is of great significance and urgency.

To date, considerable laboratory-based analytical methods have been successfully developed and employed for pesticide detection, including liquid/gas chromatography (HPLC/GC), HPLC/GC–mass spectrometry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, fluorescent, colorimetric, and electrochemistry assays (Harischandra et al, 2021; Liu et al., 2021; Xie et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2023). Most recently, ever-increasing attentions have been focused on fluorescent technology, especially for carbon dots (CDs)–based fluorescent system, due to the characteristics of easy preparation, fascinating photoluminescent property, high sensitivity, fast response, reliable visible results, and on-site detection (Yu et al., 2022; Zhu, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2022a). And most of the sensing strategies relied on the inhibition of pesticides to enzyme (e.g., acetyl cholinesterase, butyryl cholinesterase, esterase, alkaline phosphatase) (Fan et al., 2022; Mishra et al., 2022; Tong et al., 2023a). Although substantial achievements have been obtained, some challenges still existed, such as (i) enzymes are expensive and susceptible to environment, and the inactivation of enzyme will affect the accuracy and sensitivity, (ii) broad-spectrum inhibition activities of pesticides to enzymes limit the simultaneous detection of real samples (Zhang et al., 2022). As for CDs-based enzyme-free simultaneous detection, two interesting works have been reported (Chen et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2023). Chen and co-workers mixed blue-emission CDs and red-emission copper nanoclusters to fabricate ratiometric sensing system for thiram and paraquat detection by changing the solvent environment (Chen et al., 2022). Yang et al. developed a dual-emission CDs (blue and red emissions) to detect parathion-methyl and glyphosate through dual-channel emissions by inner filter effect and static quenching effect, respectively (Yang et al., 2023). Notably, by comparison with single-emission system, ratiometric detection can effectively enhance stability, accuracy, and sensitivity through self-calibration, and provide rich color changes for visible detection (Abbasi-Moayed, Bigdeli, & Hormozi-Nezhad, 2022; Shen et al., 2022). Nevertheless, there is still a great need to fabricate reliable, efficient, and portable on-site ratiometric fluorescent methods for warning-early pesticide residues.

Fluorescent response can be easily captured by a portable smartphone with high resolution and rapidly analyzed by professional app to realize sample-to-answer analysis (Shen et al., 2022). Furthermore, microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD) possesses attractive features including small sample/reagent consumption, rapid prototyping, easy operation, portability, and cost-efficiency (Campo, Vaquer, & Rica, 2022). Encouragingly, μPAD in combination with camera-readout and app-analysis is breeding innovative sensing approaches in on-site detection for food safety evaluation, environmental monitoring, and clinical diagnosis (Ling et al., 2023; Placer, Lavilla, Francisco, & Bendicho, 2023; Tong et al., 2023b). As for on-site detection of pesticides, Zhang et al. reported a 2D μPAD integrated smartphone platform for on-site visual quantification of glyphosate, and color varied from blue to pink to orange by CDs (blue emission) and gold nanoclusters (red emission) fluorescent system (Zhang et al., 2022). More recently, we integrated 3D μPAD and CDs (red emission)/2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP, yellow emission) system for on-site visual sensing of organophosphorus pesticides with distinguishable color alteration from red to yellow (Tong et al., 2023b). However, these single-channel μPADs can only detect a target pesticide at a time. With smart design of reaction and multi-channel μPAD system, multiple analytes can be simultaneously detected (Budsakorn, Akarapong, Wipark, & Purim, 2022; Li et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2022b). Up to now, to our best knowledge, developing a 3D μPAD system for on-site simultaneous detection of pesticides is not reported and highly desirable.

In this study, hinge-like paper-based dual-channel enzyme-free ratiometric fluorescent microfluidic platform has been developed for simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate. As depicted in Scheme 1 , carbaryl would be hydrolyzed into blue-emission 1-naphthol (1-NP, Em. 470 nm) under alkali environment, while glyphosate can coordinate with Cu2+, and then the redundant free Cu2+ would oxidize o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to yellow-emission DAP (Em. 570 nm). Then green-emission CDs (Em. 510 nm) were introduced to fabricate ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for visual detection of carbaryl (from green to blue) and glyphosate (from yellow to green). Based on the reaction system, a hinge-like dual-channel and double-layer 3D μPAD with prestored reagents was designed. The fluorescent images on the 2nd layer were captured by smartphone, and the color information was processed by self-programmed app. Excitedly, comparing with the reported pesticides detection systems, our developed strategy possesses tremendous advantages: (i) dual-channel 3D μPAD design ensured the independent reaction zones for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate without any crosstalk, (ii) enzyme-free detection system provided gentle reaction conditions for more adaptability of on-site detection, (iii) distinct color transition from ratiometric sensing can achieve a good visual detection effect with high accuracy, and (iv) smartphone in combination with self-programmed app provided an effective implementation for point-of-care testing with sample-in-result-out. Obviously, the developed system shows promising potentials in on-site simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate.
Details of reagents and apparatus are described in the Electronic Supplementary Materials.

Green-emission CDs were synthesized as previously reported with minor modifications (Kong et al., 2017). A starting material, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid, was isolated from ginkgo leaves wastes (details are presented in the Electronic Supplementary Materials and Fig. S1). 4-Hydroxybenzoic acid (345 mg) and ethylenediamine (0.25 mL) were mixed with ultra-pure water (25 mL), which was sonicated for 10 min and then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon autoclave to heat at 170 ℃ for 11 h. The reaction solution was then cooled down to room temperature, filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane, and dialyzed (500 D) against water for 24 h. Finally, green-emission CDs were lyophilized and kept at 4 ℃ in dark for further use.

Hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD was prepared as follows. In brief, 60 mm × 50 mm sized rectangle of non-fluorescent Waterman No.4 filter paper was selected as the substrate, which was folded into two-layer flow passage with the size of 60 mm × 25 mm (the 1st layer for sample loading and reaction, and the 2nd layer for reaction and detection) (Scheme 1 and Fig. S2A). Hydrophobic barriers of dual-channel and reaction/detection zones on paper were plotted with a commercially available black oil-pen along the edge of metal molds with specific shape and size according to our previous reported method (Zhu et al., 2022b). On the 1st layer, a sample zone located at the center (diameter, 8 mm), which followed by two I-shaped paths (9 mm × 3 mm) and reaction zones (diameter, 6 mm). On the 2nd layer, two equal detection zones (diameter, 6 mm) were prepared, which overlapped with the reaction zones by folding two layers. Reaction zones of channels A and B on the 1st layer were pre-loaded with NaOH (4.0 mg/mL, 5 μL) and Cu2+ (0.4 mM, 5 μL), respectively. While for detection zones of channels A and B on the 2nd layer, CDs (1.5 mg/mL, 5 μL) and CDs (1.0 mg/mL, 5 μL) were pre-deposited, respectively. Finally, the prepared hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD was naturally dried at room temperature, sealed, and stored away from light and moisture at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate using 3D μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: Carbaryl and glyphosate solutions with different concentrations or real sample solutions (30 μL) were added at sample zone on the 1st layer, which was equally split into reaction zones of channels A and B to react with preloaded NaOH and Cu2+ at 45 °C for 5 min, respectively. After that, the 1st layer was folded onto the 2nd layer, PBS solution (5 μL, 10 mM, pH 10) and OPD in PBS solution (0.1 M, 5 μL, pH 7) were dropped on the reaction zones of channels A and B, respectively, on the 1st layer. At this time, the produced 1-NP and unreacted Cu2+ were transferred to the detection zones on the 2nd layer. After another incubation for 15 min at 45 °C, fluorescent images on the 2nd layers were captured via smartphone under 365 nm excitation. In order to obtain robust fluorescence images on-site and ensure the effectiveness and reliability of the results, a self-made portable device including smartphone, UV lamp, and dark box were fabricated (Fig. S3). Finally, self-programmed app (https://nongdu.ycqnb.top/) was utilized to acquire RGB (red, green, and blue channels) values of fluorescent images and analyze relationships between RGB values and carbaryl/glyphosate concentrations. The B/G ratio to carbaryl concentration and the (G + B)/R ratio to glyphosate concentration relationships were achieved for accurate quantitative detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Food/herbal samples (i.e., pear, cherry tomato, Semen Coicis, and Semen Euryales) were selected to verify the practicability of the proposed system in real samples. Firstly, 10.0 g of each sample was sprayed with different amounts of carbaryl and glyphosate mixtures. After dried at room temperature, the spiked samples were ultrasonically extracted with ethanol:water (50 mL, 3:7, v/v) for 30 min (Picó, Font, Ruiz, & Fernández, 2006; Yang et al., 2023). Subsequently, the obtained extract solutions were filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove undissolved substances, and concentrated to dry under vacuum, which were then dispersed with ethanol:water solution (1 mL, 3:7, v/v). Finally, 30 μL of the solution was drawn and dropped on the sample zone on the 1st layer, and the following experimental procedures kept the same as those mentioned above using 3D μPAD for simultaneous detection. Three groups of parallel experiments (n = 3) were performed for each sample to obtain average values and standard deviations.

Wastes from food/herb contain numerous compounds, which have been demonstrated as excellent raw materials for CDs synthesis with obvious advantages such as high abundance, low cost, and reduced pollution (Atchudan et al., 2022). Ginkgo leaves, a tradition Chinese medicine, have been used to purify bioactive ginkgo flavonoids and terpene lactones for clinical applications (Guo, Wu, Jiang, & Wang, 2022). As a result, considerable quantities of ginkgo leaves wastes were discarded, which, however, still contained rich chemical compounds (Wu et al., 2022). Herein, 4-hydroxybenzoic acid was isolated and purified from ginkgo leaves wastes (Fig. S1), which can be used to prepare blue-emission CDs (Fig. S4). Furthermore, with the addition of nitrogen-containing compound (i.e., ethylenediamine), green-emission CDs were obtained (Fig. S4). The obtained green-emission CDs presented quantum yield of 6.6% (calculated using quinine sulfate as the reference).

The successful synthesis of CDs was verified by a variety of characterization methods. The morphological characterization of CDs was studied through TEM. CDs show well-dispersed spherical shape with average particle size of 6.77 ± 1.56 nm (Fig. 1
A). The high-resolution TEM clearly presents the crystal lattice with lattice spacing at 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 1A), which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphite carbons (Tong et al., 2023a). XRD pattern has a broad diffraction peak at 23.0° (Fig. S5), disclosing the sp2 hybridization of amorphous core in CDs (Macairan et al., 2022). FT-IR was applied to explore the surface functional groups of CDs, which includes the stretching vibrations of OH/NH (3283 cm−1), CH (2898 cm−1), CO/CC/CN (1640, 1578 cm−1), CN (1424, 1314 cm−1), and COC (1158, 1109 and 1030 cm−1) (Fig. 1B). The element composition was garnered via XPS. According to XPS full scan spectrum, peaks at 532.08 eV, 399.08 eV, and 285.08 eV indicate that CDs include O (16.1%), N (16.9%), and C (67.0%) elements (Fig. 1C). High-resolution XPS spectrum for O1s demonstrates two peaks at 532.38 and 531.68 eV, which are attributed to CO and CO (Fig. S6A). Whereas N1s spectrum has binding energy peaks at 400.68 eV for graphitic-N and 399.18 eV for pyrrolic-N (Fig. S6B). And for C 1 s spectrum, peaks at 288.36, 285.78, 285.18, and 284.28 eV suggest the existence of CN/CO, CO, CN, and CC/CC, (Fig. S6C). The XPS results agree with those of FT-IR, and both illustrate the presence of abundant carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface of CDs.

Optical performance of CDs was evaluated before application tests. As shown in Fig. 1D, UV–vis spectrum of CDs displays two characteristic peaks at about 250 and 390 nm, which could be ascribed to the π → π* transition of CC and n → π* transition of CN/CO. Fluorescent measurement manifests that the maximum emission peak of CDs locates at 512 nm under 412 nm excitation (Fig. 1D). Emission peaks shift slightly from 509 to 520 nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 335 to 425 nm (Fig. S7). The excitation-dependent behavior of CDs might be aroused by the different particle sizes and intricate surface states (Zhu et al., 2022b).

Optical stability of CDs is remarkably related to the precision and repeatability. Thus, the performance of CDs at different pH, ionic strength, storage time, and light irradiation were investigated. As depicted in Fig. S8, CDs possess outstanding photostability under pH from 6.0 to 10.0, NaCl concentration from 0.1 to 0.8 M, and continuous UV irradiation (365 nm) for 60 min. The fluorescent intensities of CDs decline with the pH values increasing from 10.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S8A) perhaps due to the dissociation of the surface functional groups (Tong et al., 2023a). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity barely fluctuates after 6 months storage under 4 °C. Therefore, the prominent optical stability of CDs ensured the accurate and reliable sensing property in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operation principles for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate
ce:section.ce:para: In light of the superiority, CDs can notably improve performance of sensors, which then has been fabricated as efficient fluorescent indicators for sensing multiple organic and inorganic analytes, not to say pesticides (Umapathi et al., 2022). Previous reported CDs-based strategies for simultaneous pesticides detection were performed in solution by spectrofluorometer, and the results were obtained by two operation experiments through changing the solvent environment (Chen et al., 2022) or the excitation and emission (Yang et al., 2023). Thus, it is still a challenging work to develop moreelaboratemethodsfor simultaneous pesticides detection. Interestingly, methyl isocyanate group of carbaryl can be hydrolyzed under alkali environment by SN2 substitution reaction to produce 1-NP ([M−H]– 143.0490, –4.89 ppm, 59 Da less than that of carbaryl) (Scheme 1, Fig. S9). When the reaction pH value is set at pH 12, the hydrolysis efficiency reaches up to 96.8 % (Fig. S10). A turn-on blue emission at 470 nm for 1-NP is observed, and the fluorescent intensity at 470 nm increases with the increasement of carbaryl concentration (Fig. S11A). On the other hand, glyphosate can complex with Cu2+ through phosphonyl, imino, and carboxyl groups (Scheme 1). After that, free Cu2+ oxidizes OPD into DAP with yellow emission at 570 nm, and as a result, the fluorescent intensity at 570 nm decreases with the increasement of glyphosate concentration (Fig. S11B). Then we speculated that ratiometric detection of carbaryl and glyphosate can be realized by the introduction of green-emission CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A–B and Fig. S12, obvious color variations are observed for carbaryl detection from green to blue, and for glyphosate detection from yellow to green, which can be visualized by naked eye. F510/LogF470 values show good linear correlation to carbaryl concentrations (0.1–50.0 μM), and excellent linear relationship is observed between F570/F510 values and glyphosate concentrations (1.0–40.0 μM) (Fig. 2C–D), indicating the feasibility of ratiometric detection of carbaryl and glyphosate. The limit of detection (LOD) values was calculated to be 0.084 μM for carbaryl and 0.20 μM for glyphosate on the basis of 3δ/S (δ and S were the standard deviation of blank samples and the slop of linear equation, respectively, n = 12). However, the solution system cannot realize the simultaneous ratiometric detection in solution because of the crosstalk. Notably, fabrication of 3D μPAD with two parallel channels could meet the need for two different reaction systems for on-site simultaneous ratiometric detection with one sample injection (Scheme 1). NaOH, Cu2+, and CDs were preloaded on the 3D μPAD, and reactions for each channel occurred without any crosstalk. For channel A on the 1st layer, added carbaryl was hydrolyzed into 1-NP by preloaded NaOH, and then 1-NP was transferred onto the 2nd layer by PBS solution for ratiometric detection of carbaryl by folding the 3D μPAD. While for channel B on the 1st layer, added glyphosate was firstly combined with preloaded Cu2+. Subsequently, free Cu2+ was transferred onto the 2nd layer by dropping OPD solution. On the 2nd layer, Cu2+ oxidized OPD into DAP, which mixed with CDs for ratiometric detection of glyphosate. The fluorescent images for detection zones on the 2nd layer can be conveniently obtained by portable smartphone. Obviously, a facile, efficient, and reliable method was developed for point-of-care testing of carbaryl and glyphosate concurrently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for carbaryl and glyphosate sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent spectra of CDs with the increasing concentrations of carbaryl and glyphosate were presented in Fig. S12. Obviously, fluorescent intensities of CDs at 510 nm increase considerably with the increasing concentrations of 1-NP hydrolyzed from carbaryl and decrease significantly after the production of DAP. Obviously, CDs quenched the fluorescence of 1-NP, whereas DAP quenched the fluorescence of CDs. Large spectral overlap exists between absorption spectrum of CDs (Abs. maximum 390 nm) and excitation spectrum of 1-NP (Ex. maximum 330 nm), between absorption spectrum of DAP (Abs. maximum 415 nm) and CDs (Ex. maximum 412 nm) (Fig. 3 A–B). Thus, sensing mechanism mainly came from inner-filter effect (IFE). Meanwhile, fluorescence lifetime assays were performed (Fig. 3C–D). A high degree of consistence of the fluorescence decay curves further suggested the occurrence of IFE. In addition, UV–vis spectra of CDs + 1-NP and CDs + DPA were investigated (Fig. S13). No new absorption peaks were observed, which ruled out the static quenching effect. As a result, the fluorescence sensing mechanism of CDs to 1-NP/DAP is assigned as IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate by 3D μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: The optimum experimental parameters were firstly investigated to achieve precise, efficient, and stable performance of the hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate. Firstly, red ink solution was used to optimize the injection volume on 3D μPAD. As displayed in Fig. S2 B–C, it takes 30 μL of sample solution to flow from sample zone to reaction zone and 5 μL of solution for layer-to-layer penetration, and at this circumstance, red ink solution distributes uniformly on the hydrophilic zones.

Subsequently, pivotal factors (i.e., concentrations of NaOH and CDs, reaction time, and reaction temperature) for carbaryl detection on 3D μPAD were investigated (Fig. S14). Results indicated that carbaryl can be hydrolyzed quickly (less than 1 min) and stably within 25 min when the concentration of NaOH reached 4.0 mg/mL, and the reaction was seldom affected with the temperature between 25 °C and 45 °C. It was turned out that the hydrolysis of carbaryl in strong alkaline environment was a quite fast process. The fluorescence intensity of hydrolysis product 1-NP enhances significantly when pH is greater than 10 because of the dissociation of hydroxyl group (Fig. S15A). In consideration of the stability of CDs to pH (Fig. S8A), PBS solution (5 μL, 10 mM, pH 10) was selected to transfer produced 1-NP from 1st layer to the 2nd layer. Different concentrations of CDs (1.0, 1.5, 2.0 mg/mL) were located on 3D μPAD to investigate the color change in carbaryl (50 and 1100 μM). According to Fig. S16A and C, noticeable color changes and higher linear slope are obtained when concentration of CDs is set at 1.5 mg/mL. Under the optimal conditions, noticeable color change from green to blue on the 2nd layer of 3D μPAD is found as the concentration of carbaryl increasing from 50.00 to 1100 μM, and an excellent linear relationship is established between B/G ratio and logarithm of carbaryl concentration (R2 = 0.9925) (Fig. 4 A). The linear equation is y = 0.1968 × + 0.5998, and the LOD was calculated to be 1.11 μM. Simultaneously, for sensitive detection of glyphosate, concentrations of Cu2+, OPD, and CDs, reaction time of Cu2+–OPD and Cu2+–glyphosate, reaction temperature, and detection pH were evaluated and optimized on 3D μPAD. As exhibited in Fig. S15B, produced DAP is stable when pH values vary from 5 to 7, thus PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7) was selected as the reaction environment. With the increasing of Cu2+ and OPD concentrations, more DAP with fluorescent yellow color was produced, and finally Cu2+ at 0.4 mM and OPD at 100 mM are selected (Fig. S17A and B). Reaction temperature affected the reaction rate, and the reaction completes within 15 min when the temperature is set at 45 °C (Fig. S17C and D). While for Cu2+ and glyphosate complexation at 45 °C, 5 min is enough (Fig. S17E and F). Concentrations of CDs can also influence the color change and linear slop in glyphosate detection process. Fig. S16B and C indicates that the concentration of CDs can be deposited as 1.0 mg/mL. Using the optimal parameters, distinguishable color variations from yellow to green on the 2nd layer of 3D μPAD were observed with the increment of glyphosate concentration from 5.00 to 600 μM (inset in Fig. 4B). (G + B)/R value shows a good linear correlation to glyphosate concentration (y = 0.0094 × + 2.0472, R2 = 0.9929) with LOD at 0.63 μM (3σ/S) (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, the CIE chromaticity diagram confirmed the feasibility of detecting carbaryl and glyphosate with naked eyes (Fig. 4C and D). Stability and repeatability were further investigated for carbaryl (100, 400, and 700 μM) and glyphosate (100, 200, 300 μM) detection. Results indicated that satisfactory precision was achieved with the relative standard deviation values at 1.44–5.54 % and 2.88–7.89 % for intra-day (three parallel detection) and inter-day (5-day span) variations. Most colorimetric and fluorometric methods for carbaryl and glyphosate detection were single-channel assay for single-target detection with experimental instruments (Table S1 and S2). Apparently, our strategy contained exceptional performance in simultaneous ratiometric detection, comparative high sensitivity, wide linear range, on-site detection, and low cost, which was suitable for daily point-of-care applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the practicability of the developed hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD strategy for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples, interferences experiments were firstly evaluated. As shown in Fig. S18 and S19, common metal ions (Na+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Al3+), amino acids (l-phenylalanine, l-Phe; l-leucine, l-Leu), and pesticides (parathion-methyl, parathion, carbendazim, and thiram) with concentration at 1000 μM presented negligible influence on B/G and (G + B)/R vales for carbaryl and glyphosate detection. Furthermore, there was no crosstalk between carbaryl and glyphosate assays due to the two individual reaction channels (A and B) of the developed platform. The outstanding selectivity exhibited great potential for simultaneous detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real samples.

In practical applications, two kinds of food (i.e., pear and cherry tomato) and two kinds of medicine-food herbs (i.e., Semen Coicis and Semen Euryales) were selected. As illustrated in Table 1 , no carbaryl and glyphosate residues were found in real samples. After spraying with different concentrations of carbaryl and glyphosate, satisfactory recoveries (91.00–106.4% for carbaryl, 92.64–107.2% for glyphosate) with relative standard deviation (RSD) values less than 8.43% were presented. Detection results by our developed platform were consistent with those detected by HPLC-based system. The considerable and excellent performance demonstrated that the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system based on hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD was applicable for on-site simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate in real food/herbal samples.

In summary, by combining the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system with a hinge-like dual-channel 3D μPAD-based portable detection system, we established a facile, enzyme-free, and cost-effective platform for simultaneous visual detection of carbaryl and glyphosate with high sensitivity and selectivity. Due to the IEF between CDs and 1-NP/DAP, green-emissive CDs quenched the blue emission of 1-NP, while yellow-emissive DAP quenched the green emission of CDs. Thus, two ratiometric fluorescent systems were achieved with obvious fluorescent color changes. Two channels on the designed 3D μPAD simultaneously responded to carbaryl and glyphosate-related reactions independently without crosstalk. The developed strategy can also be extended to simultaneously detect other pesticides through designing rational reaction systems. So, we expected this work might open up a novel avenue for on-site visual detection of multi-pesticides.

Yangyu Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Chaoying Tong: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Rongrong Zhou: Validation, Data curation, Visualization, Conceptualization. Ziyi Qin: Data curation, Conceptualization, Methodology. Jinju Xu: Data curation, Validation, Visualization. Chunhui Liao: Formal analysis, Investigation, Validation. Shuihan Zhang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shuyun Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Ying Guo: Project administration, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Key Research and Development Projects in Hunan Province, China (2021NK2024), the Key Research Program of Hunan Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine (C2022042, 2021SK2012), and the Special Project of “National Chinese Medica (CMM) Resources Survey” ([2017]66).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137127.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
